mode: 25 rps, 2 minutes per connector, 3000 records each, 50 rotating unique ~1MB payloads, ByteArrayConverter, connectors isolated one at a time

connectors.name: mongo
connectors.producer: 3000 records, 24.999583 rps, 24.79 MB/sec, avg 8.36 ms, p50 8 ms, p95 11 ms, p99 16 ms, p99.9 64 ms, max 453 ms
connectors.transform: cold cumulative p50 8.323 ms, p95 10.993 ms, p99 13.355 ms, max 30.416 ms; warm post-60s windowMax avg 15.832 ms, max 72.493 ms
connectors.memory: Connect around 6.72 GiB during monitor tail

connectors.name: postgres
connectors.producer: 3000 records, 24.998750 rps, 24.79 MB/sec, avg 7.06 ms, p50 7 ms, p95 8 ms, p99 10 ms, p99.9 23 ms, max 211 ms
connectors.transform: cold cumulative p50 1.755 ms, p95 5.979 ms, p99 8.861 ms, max 15.715 ms; warm post-60s windowMax avg 9.096 ms, max 36.288 ms
connectors.memory: Connect around 6.76 GiB during monitor tail

connectors.name: s3
connectors.producer: 3000 records, 25.003334 rps, 24.80 MB/sec, avg 6.99 ms, p50 7 ms, p95 8 ms, p99 9 ms, p99.9 23 ms, max 201 ms
connectors.transform: recent bounded probe p50 ~4.6-4.8 ms, p95 ~6.6-7.0 ms, p99 ~7.7-8.3 ms, max observed 22.581 ms
connectors.memory: Connect around 6.80 GiB during monitor tail
connectors.note: Residual lag is consistent with S3 sink flush.size=10 and no time-based rotation for sparse partition tails.

notes: All three connectors were converted/kept on ByteArrayConverter for the run.
Mongo and Postgres drained to zero lag and wrote 1988 successful sink rows/docs; 4048 field-level DLQ events were expected from intentionally bad fields.
S3 wrote objects successfully but left 179 records as consumer lag because S3 only commits offsets with file commits; add rotate.interval.ms or lower flush.size for demo-grade drain behavior.
Cold-inclusive and warm-only are not identical metrics: current probe logs cumulative p99 plus rolling windowMaxMs, not rolling warm p99.
